prompt.title: Welcome!
prompt.description: Looks like it's your first time here. If you're new to radio astronomy, the guided observation will walk you through how to detect the hydrogen line. Otherwise, take a tour of the controls or simply explore!

trace.title: The trace
trace.description: This graph displays the total signal received by the radio telescope over the last 60 seconds.

hydrogenMarker.title: Reference Frequency
hydrogenMarker.description: Neutral hydrogen atoms in a lab will emit radio waves at exactly 1420.406 MHz. This can be used as a reference to determine doppler shift.

measurements.title: The measurements
measurements.description: Information about any detected hydrogen signal will be displayed here, including its relative velocity.

baselineCorrection.title: Baseline correction
baselineCorrection.description: Receivers have their own bandpass shape even with no signal. Capturing a baseline on empty sky subtracts that shape, and any local interference, so the hydrogen line stands out.

waterfall.title: The waterfall
waterfall.description: Open this to see signal strength stacked over time. A genuine hydrogen line shows up as a persistent vertical streak; random noise flickers and never lines up.

welcome.title: Welcome
welcome.description: This is a short tour of the telescope control panel. Use Next/Back to step through, or press Esc to exit at any time.

statusBar.title: Status bar
statusBar.description: Connection state, telescope time, and - when you are in control - your session timer live up here.

spectrum.title: Spectrum
spectrum.description: Live FFT from the SDR. The rolling average is configured on the server; watch for the hydrogen line near 1420 MHz.

manualJog.title: Manual jog
manualJog.description: Press and hold a direction to nudge the dish. The center button is an emergency stop for all motion, and the speed presets set how fast it moves.

gotoTarget.title: Go to a target
gotoTarget.description: Type a target's RA (hours) and Dec (degrees) - the coordinates any star chart or catalogue lists - then hit the arrow to drive the dish there automatically.

skyMapDesktop.title: Sky map
skyMapDesktop.description: Live view of the sky from the telescope's location. Click a target on the map, then hit the Slew button that appears to point the dish there.

skyMapMobile.title: Sky map
skyMapMobile.description: Live view of the sky from the telescope's location. Tap a target on the map, then hit Slew to point the dish there.

telemetry.title: Telemetry
telemetry.description: Encoder positions, motor currents, voltages, and safety state. Watch here if a move feels wrong - overcurrent trips show up immediately.

done.title: You are set
done.description: That covers the main sections. You can re-run this tour anytime from the Help button in the top bar.

intro.title: Observe hydrogen
intro.description: We'll point the dish at two patches of sky and look for the faint radio glow given off by hydrogen gas at 1420 MHz - the most common element in the galaxy. The whole thing takes about three minutes.

liveSignal.title: Live radio signal
liveSignal.description: This yellow trace shows the radio energy the dish is hearing right now. The vertical green marker at 1420.4 MHz is where pure hydrogen emits. We're looking for a small bump that lines up with - or shifts slightly off - that marker.

emptySky.title: Aim at empty sky
emptySky.description: First we point somewhere with very little hydrogen, to learn what the receiver sounds like on its own. We picked the sky near the North Galactic Pole - looking "up" out of our galaxy, where almost no hydrogen gas lies in the way.

saveBaseline.title: Save what 'empty' looks like
saveBaseline.description: Click "Set up baseline" below. This stores the current trace as a reference, so when we slew somewhere with real hydrogen the signal pops out clearly against this flat background. Then press Next.

milkyWay.title: Aim at the Milky Way
milkyWay.description: Now we'll swing to a thick part of our galaxy's disk, in Cygnus. There's a huge column of hydrogen gas between us and the far side of the galaxy, all emitting at 1420 MHz. Watch the yellow trace once the dish settles - a bump should grow near the green marker.

done.title: You did it
done.description: The bump you see is hydrogen gas tens of thousands of light-years away. If it sits slightly off the green marker, that gas is moving toward or away from us - the Doppler effect - and the velocity readout under the chart tells you how fast. Slew elsewhere any time to compare.

intro.body: Radio receivers have their own "shape" - even with no signal at all, the spectrum from a real antenna isn't flat. Baseline correction stores a reference trace of that shape and divides it out of live data, so real features like the hydrogen line stand out instead of getting lost in the bandpass curve.
intro.prompt: We'll capture one by pointing somewhere quiet and freezing the trace.

pick.title: Pick an empty patch of sky
pick.description: Use the sky map to choose somewhere quiet - high in the sky, away from the Sun and the shaded Milky Way band (clicks inside it are blocked). Click a point on the map to load it as your target, then hit the Slew button to drive the dish there.

capture.body: Once the dish has finished slewing onto your empty patch, trigger the capture. The telescope keeps observing the whole time - it averages a full integration window of this live spectrum into your baseline, then applies it. No need to time it yourself.
capture.liveCaption: This is the live spectrum right now - the receiver's bandpass shape we're about to measure. The dashed line marks 1420.4 MHz.
capture.liveCaptionActive: Integrating... averaging this live spectrum into your baseline. Watch it settle as the noise averages down.
capture.liveWaiting: Waiting for the first spectrum frame...
capture.countdownPrefix: Capturing... averaging the live spectrum over
capture.noFrame: No spectrum frame received yet - wait a moment for the first one to arrive.

done.body: Done - the live spectrum is now drawn with this baseline corrected. The bandpass curve should flatten out, so anything left poking up is a real feature in the sky rather than the receiver's shape.
done.meta: Look around 1420.4 MHz: a narrow bump that persists as a vertical streak in the waterfall is the hydrogen line.
